summary: King of England (r. 1272-1307), nicknamed "Longshanks" for his height and the "Hammer of the Scots" (Malleus Scotorum) for his wars against Scotland. Clues identify him chiefly as the antagonist of William Wallace, whom he defeated at the Battle of Falkirk and later executed. As a prince he helped crush the Second Barons' War at the Battle of Evesham; as king he completed the conquest of Wales (building the "Iron Ring" of castles with architect James of Saint George), fought the Wars of Scottish Independence (Battle of Dunbar, seizure of the Stone of Scone, the Great Cause over the Scottish succession), expelled the Jews of England in 1290, summoned the Model Parliament, and passed major legal reforms including the Statute of Quia Emptores. He mourned his wife Eleanor of Castile with the Eleanor Crosses and survived an assassination attempt during the Ninth Crusade.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: Edward I is identified in giveaways by his two nicknames — "Longshanks," for his unusually long legs, and the "Hammer of the Scots" (Malleus Scotorum), for his campaigns against Scotland — and above all as the historical antagonist of William Wallace, whom he defeated and later executed. He succeeded his father, Henry III, and questions often summarize his reign as conquering Wales while ultimately failing to subdue Scotland.

clues.clue: Nicknamed "Longshanks" for his long legs and the "Hammer of the Scots" (Malleus Scotorum) after his wars against Scotland
examples: name this king nicknamed the Hammer of the Scots.
name this king nicknamed Longshanks who conquered Wales.

clues.clue: Best known in giveaways as the historical antagonist of William Wallace, whom he ultimately captured and executed
examples: name this English king, best known as the antagonist of William Wallace.
This king expelled the Jews of England and later executed William Wallace.

clues.clue: Succeeded his father, Henry III, as King of England
examples: name this successor of Henry III, an English king known as Longshanks.

clues.clue: Summarized in giveaways as the king who conquered Wales but ultimately failed to subdue Scotland
examples: name this English king who conquered Wales but failed to subdue Scotland.

clues.clue: Once threw his youngest daughter Elizabeth's golden coronet into a fire during a fit of anger while planning her wedding to John I, Count of Holland
examples: This king once got so upset during the planning of his youngest daughter's wedding to John I, the Count of Holland, that he threw Elizabeth's golden coronet into a fire.

works.name: Conquest of Wales
works.description: Edward I's Welsh campaigns culminated in the 1282-83 conquest that ended Welsh independence: after Llywelyn ap Gruffudd, "Llywelyn the Last," was killed at the Battle of Orewin Bridge, Edward annexed the principality and ringed it with a chain of new castles designed by the Savoyard architect James of Saint George, formalizing his control with the Statute of Rhuddlan.

clues.clue: Completed the conquest of Wales after Llywelyn ap Gruffudd ("Llywelyn the Last") was killed at the Battle of Orewin Bridge in 1282, having been provoked in part by Llywelyn's marriage to Eleanor de Montfort
examples: After the 1282 death of Llywelyn the Last, this English king completed the conquest of Wales.
Provoked when an opposing ruler married Eleanor de Montfort, this campaign ended with that ruler's death at the Battle of Orewin Bridge.

clues.clue: Employed the Savoyard architect James of Saint George to build an "Iron Ring" of castles around North Wales, including Beaumaris, Conwy, Caernarfon, Harlech, Flint, and Rhuddlan
examples: This king employed the Savoyard architect James of Saint George, and built castles such as Beaumaris on the island of Anglesey.
He erected the castles of Conwy and Caernarfon after putting down a Welsh revolt.

clues.clue: Formally annexed Wales with the Statute of Rhuddlan (1284), imposing English-speaking sheriffs and common law on new counties
examples: After defeating both Dafydd ap Gruffydd and his older brother Llywelyn, this king issued the Statute of Rhuddland, formally annexing Wales.
At the end of this process, English-speaking sheriffs enforcing common law were imposed on settler-populated counties carved out by the Statute of Rhuddlan.

clues.clue: First granted the title "Prince of Wales" to the heir to the English throne after completing the conquest
examples: This king, nicknamed for his lengthy legs, first granted the title Prince of Wales to the heir to the English throne.

clues.clue: Financed the campaign by granting the Riccardi of Lucca control of customs collections on wool exports
examples: In exchange for financing this campaign, the Riccardi of Lucca were given customs collections on wool exports.

clues.clue: Archbishop John Peckham tried to negotiate an end to the campaign; the Welsh side denounced Edward as worse than Saracens in the "Declaration of Defiance"
examples: Archbishop John Peckham tried to negotiate an end to this campaign, but the opposing side denounced a ruler as worse than Saracens in the "Declaration of Defiance."

clues.clue: Used Marcher Lord castles as forward bases for expeditions into mountainous Snowdonia
examples: Its victor used Marcher Lord castles as forward bases for expeditions into mountainous Snowdonia.

clues.clue: According to legend, completion of the conquest inspired the "last bard" to issue a curse before jumping off a cliff
examples: According to legend, this action's completion inspired the "last bard" to issue a curse before jumping off a cliff.

clues.clue: Replaced the earlier Treaty of Shrewsbury with the Treaty of Aberconwy after subduing the region
examples: This king replaced the Treaty of Shrewsbury with the Treaty of Aberconwy after subduing a neighboring region.

works.name: Wars of Scottish Independence
works.description: After the Treaty of Birgham (arranging his son's marriage to Margaret, Maid of Norway) collapsed with Margaret's death, Edward was called in to judge the Scottish succession dispute (the "Great Cause"), then used the resulting instability to invade, defeat, and dominate Scotland, seizing its coronation stone and later crushing William Wallace's rebellion.

clues.clue: Personally led English forces to victory over William Wallace at the Battle of Falkirk (1298)
examples: This king also won the Battle of Falkirk.
The Jews were expelled from England by this ruler, who was victorious at the Battle of Falkirk after being defeated at Stirling Bridge by William Wallace.

clues.clue: Judged the succession dispute over the vacant Scottish throne (the "Great Cause"), involving claimants including John Balliol and John Hastings, then installed and later imprisoned Balliol as a puppet king
examples: This man was brought in to judge a succession crisis involving men like John Hastings in the Great Cause.
This king seized the Stone of Destiny during a war where he imprisoned John Balliol.

clues.clue: Won the Battle of Dunbar (1296) after the Treaty of Birgham, which had arranged his son's marriage to Margaret, Maid of Norway, collapsed with her death
examples: This man secured his son's marriage to Margaret, Maid of Norway in the Treaty of Birgham, but after Margaret's death he won the Battle of Dunbar.
This English king initiated the Wars of Scottish Independence and won the Battle of Dunbar in 1296.

clues.clue: Seized the Stone of Scone (Stone of Destiny), on which Scottish kings were traditionally inaugurated, and incorporated it into England's Coronation Chair
examples: He removed the stone of Scone to England, earning him the name of Hammer of the Scots.
After the Battle of Dunbar, English forces seized this object from an abbey. Scottish kings were originally inaugurated on this object, which Edward incorporated into the Coronation Chair.

clues.clue: His forces under Hugh de Cressingham were routed at the Battle of Stirling Bridge (1297), where Wallace and Andrew de Moray exploited a bottleneck at the bridge
examples: This man's forces, led by Hugh de Cressingham, were defeated in a battle in which their forces could only cross the namesake structure in twos, leading to a bottleneck in which Andrew De Moray's army could cut off reinforcements.
The Jews were expelled from England by this ruler, who was victorious at the Battle of Falkirk after being defeated at Stirling Bridge by William Wallace.

clues.clue: Later captured and executed William Wallace
examples: This king expelled the Jews of England and later executed William Wallace.

clues.clue: Provoked the original Auld Alliance (1295), a mutual-defense pact between John Balliol and Philip IV of France formed specifically to oppose him
examples: The original Auld Alliance in 1295 was an agreement between John Balliol and Philip the Fair to oppose this English monarch, known as Longshanks.

clues.clue: Constructed what is believed to be the largest trebuchet ever built, the "Warwolf," during the siege of Stirling Castle, and earlier captured Caerlaverock Castle from Herbert Maxwell
examples: This ruler constructed what is believed to be the largest trebuchet ever built, the Warwolf, during his siege of Stirling Castle.

works.name: Expulsion of the Jews from England (1290)
works.description: In 1290, Edward I ordered the expulsion of England's entire Jewish population — the first such national expulsion in medieval Europe — a decree tied to his own financial pressures, preceded by mass hangings over coin-clipping accusations, and fueled by blood libel myths, that was not reversed until Oliver Cromwell's era centuries later.

clues.clue: Ordered the expulsion of England's entire Jewish population in 1290, a religious minority forced to leave partly for practicing usury
examples: name this action ordered by Edward I, in which a religious minority that practiced usury was forced to leave England.
In 1290, anti-Semitism in England culminated with this monarch's expulsion of the Jews.

clues.clue: The decree was tied to his own money problems and preceded by the hanging of hundreds of people accused of "coin-clipping"
examples: He also led a campaign culminating in battles at Stirling Bridge and Falkirk, and his money problems led him to expel the Jews.
This action was partly inspired by the deaths of people like William of Norwich and Little Saint Hugh of Lincoln, and it was preceded by the hanging of hundreds of people accused of coin-clipping.

clues.clue: Partly inspired by blood libel myths surrounding the deaths of William of Norwich and Little Saint Hugh of Lincoln
examples: This action was partly inspired by the deaths of people like William of Norwich and Little Saint Hugh of Lincoln.

clues.clue: Before the expulsion, officials were ordered to seal up the "archae," community chests used to store Jewish moneylenders' bonds
examples: Before this event, officials were ordered to seal up the archae, community chests that were used to store bonds.

clues.clue: One ship captain, Henry Adrian, abandoned Jewish passengers on a sandbar to drown at high tide
examples: During this event, Henry Adrian had the passengers on his ship disembark on a sandbar, where he left them to drown at high tide.

clues.clue: Archbishop John le Romeyne threatened excommunication against anyone who harassed the departing Jewish population
examples: Those who harassed the participants in this event were threatened with excommunication by Archbishop John le Romeyne.

clues.clue: The Domus Conversorum ("House of Converts") was eventually repurposed as a home for clerks after the expulsion
examples: The Domus Conversorum was eventually converted to a home for clerks after this event.

clues.clue: Was not reversed until Oliver Cromwell's era, when he sought to resettle wealthy Jewish traders from Amsterdam in London
examples: This action was reversed by Oliver Cromwell, who wanted to resettle wealthy traders from Amsterdam in London.

works.name: Model Parliament & Legal Reforms
works.description: Edward I's domestic legacy rests on a set of landmark legal and parliamentary reforms — most famously the 1295 Model Parliament, which broadened representation, and the Statute of Quia Emptores, which helped dismantle feudal land tenure — alongside more contentious moves like an unpopular wool tax and a showdown with the Church over clerical taxation.

clues.clue: Convoked the "Model Parliament" of 1295, which for the first time drew in representatives of the lesser clergy alongside elected representatives of the shires and boroughs
examples: He tapped lesser clergy and lower representatives to serve in his Model Parliament.

clues.clue: Passed the Statute of Quia Emptores (the Third Statute of Westminster, 1290), which banned subinfeudation and let tenants sell land freely, hastening the end of feudalism
examples: This king outlawed the practice of mortmain and undermined the structure of feudalism with the Quia Emptores, or Third Statute of Westminster.
Quia Emptores was passed during the reign of this English king, who subjugated Wales and defeated a rebellion led by William Wallace.

clues.clue: Passed the Statutes of Mortmain (1279, 1290), which banned donating land to the Church without royal license, protecting royal revenue
examples: This king outlawed the practice of mortmain and undermined the structure of feudalism with the Quia Emptores.
Both the Statutes of Mortmain and Statute of Quia Emptores were passed by this English king who intended to strengthen the monarchy that had weakened under his father Henry III.

clues.clue: Ordered the "Hundred Rolls" census (1279) to investigate corruption among local officials, shortly after returning from the Ninth Crusade
examples: This king gathered information about corrupt local officials during a census called the "Hundred Rolls".
Shortly after returning home, he ordered a census called the Hundred Rolls.

clues.clue: Clashed with Archbishop Robert Winchelsey over taxing the clergy; Winchelsey's publication of the papal bull Clericis Laicos blocked Edward's attempt to collect half of clerical revenues
examples: His attempt to collect one-half of clerical subsidies through a grant was initially permitted by Robert Winchesley, though later outlawed by the papal bull Clericis Laicos.
This ruler was opposed in his attempt to tax the clergy by Robert Winchelsey's publishing of the papal bull, Clericis laicos.

clues.clue: Imposed an unpopular wool export tax known as the "maltolt"
examples: This man passed an unpopular tax on wool called the maltolt during his reign.

clues.clue: His regent Robert Burnell used quo warranto proceedings to claim disputed royal lands back from the nobility
examples: This king's regent Robert Burnell claimed royal lands through quo warranto proceedings.

works.name: Eleanor of Castile & the Eleanor Crosses
works.description: Edward I's grief over the 1290 death of his wife, Eleanor of Castile, produced one of his most visible monuments: a line of twelve stone crosses marking the route her body traveled to burial, later fictionalized in a legend that she saved his life on crusade.

clues.clue: After Eleanor of Castile's death (1290), erected twelve stone crosses along the route her funeral procession took from Harby to Westminster Abbey
examples: this king erected crosses along the path from Harby to Westminster Abbey to mark the funeral procession of his queen, Eleanor of Castile.
This ruler ordered the construction of a line of twelve stone monuments topped with crosses to honor his late wife, Eleanor of Castile.

works.name: Second Barons' War (as Prince)
works.description: Before becoming king, Edward fought as a prince in the Second Barons' War against the baronial faction led by Simon de Montfort — losing the Battle of Lewes and being held captive before escaping to lead the royalist victory at Evesham that restored his father's power.

clues.clue: Escaped captivity to lead royalist forces to victory over Simon de Montfort at the Battle of Evesham (1265), ending the Second Barons' War
examples: This king, who as a prince fought alongside Gilbert de Clare, won the Battle of Evesham to end the Second Barons' War.
This man won the Battle of Evesham against Simon de Montfort.

clues.clue: As a young prince fighting alongside Gilbert de Clare, was captured by the baronial forces at the Battle of Lewes (1264) before escaping
examples: He was captured at the Battle of Lewes, but led his father's troops to victory at Evesham.

works.name: The Ninth Crusade & Assassination Attempt
works.description: As a prince, Edward joined the Ninth Crusade to the Holy Land, where he survived a poisoned-dagger assassination attempt by a member of the Order of Assassins and lost his seneschal in a botched surprise attack, before cutting the crusade short to return home and claim the throne.

clues.clue: Survived a poison-dagger assassination attempt by a member of the Assassins after an unsuccessful raid on the Levantine city of Qaqun; a discredited legend claims his wife Eleanor sucked the poison from his wound
examples: This king was targeted by a poison dagger wielding assassin after his unsuccessful raid on the Levantine city of Qaqun, but managed to kill the assassin during the struggle.
A discredited story claims this ruler's wife sucked poison from his arm after he was stabbed by a member of the Assassins.

clues.clue: His seneschal Luke de Tany was killed while leading a surprise attack from a pontoon bridge
examples: This king's seneschal, Luke de Tany, was killed while launching a surprise attack from a pontoon bridge.

clues.clue: Cut short his participation in the Ninth Crusade to return home upon learning of his father Henry III's death, becoming king in absentia
examples: The Ninth Crusade effectively just ended when this prince opted to return home after hearing of his father's death.

works.name: Legacy: Cultural Depictions
works.description: George Peele's Elizabethan history play dramatizes Edward I's return from crusade and his Welsh and Scottish victories, capping his story with a fabricated deathbed scandal involving Eleanor of Castile.

clues.clue: Title subject of George Peele's play The Famous Chronicle of King Edward the First, Sirnamed Edward Longshankes, with His Return from the Holy Land, which shows his defeats of Llywelyn ap Gruffudd and John Balliol and ends with Queen Elinor's confession that she did not father Joan
examples: Peele wrote a play about this king, which ends with the confession of Elinor that he did not father Joan.

comprehensive_summary: Edward I (born 1239, r. 1272-1307) was King of England, nicknamed "Longshanks" for his height and the "Hammer of the Scots" (Malleus Scotorum) for his campaigns in Scotland; giveaways identify him above all as the historical antagonist of William Wallace. As a young prince, he fought alongside Gilbert de Clare against the baronial faction in the Second Barons' War, was captured at the Battle of Lewes (1264), and then escaped to lead royalist forces to victory over Simon de Montfort at the Battle of Evesham (1265). He subsequently joined the Ninth Crusade to the Holy Land, where he survived an assassination attempt by a member of the Order of Assassins (wielding a poisoned dagger) after a failed raid on Qaqun — a discredited legend credits his wife, Eleanor of Castile, with sucking the poison from his wound — and lost his seneschal Luke de Tany in a botched pontoon-bridge attack; he cut the crusade short to return home and claim the throne after his father Henry III's death. As king, Edward completed the conquest of Wales, provoked in part when Llywelyn ap Gruffudd ("Llywelyn the Last") married Eleanor de Montfort; Llywelyn was killed at the Battle of Orewin Bridge in 1282, after which Edward annexed the principality with the Statute of Rhuddlan, financed by handing wool-export customs to the Riccardi of Lucca, and ringed North Wales with an "Iron Ring" of castles — Beaumaris, Conwy, Caernarfon, Harlech, Flint, and Rhuddlan — designed by the Savoyard architect James of Saint George. He was the first to grant the title "Prince of Wales" to the English heir. In Scotland, after the Treaty of Birgham (arranging his son's marriage to Margaret, Maid of Norway) collapsed with her death, Edward was invited to judge the Scottish succession dispute known as the Great Cause, installed John Balliol as a puppet king, then invaded, winning the Battle of Dunbar (1296) and seizing the Stone of Scone (incorporating it into England's Coronation Chair). His initial dominance provoked the original Auld Alliance between Balliol and Philip IV of France, and after his forces under Hugh de Cressingham were routed by William Wallace and Andrew de Moray at the Battle of Stirling Bridge (1297), Edward personally led English forces to victory over Wallace at the Battle of Falkirk (1298) — building the giant trebuchet "Warwolf" during a later siege of Stirling Castle — and eventually captured and executed Wallace. In 1290, Edward ordered the expulsion of England's entire Jewish population, a decision tied to his own financial pressures, preceded by mass hangings over coin-clipping accusations, and partly fueled by blood libel myths surrounding the deaths of William of Norwich and Little Saint Hugh of Lincoln; officials sealed the Jewish community's "archae" record-chests beforehand, one ship captain abandoned Jewish passengers to drown on a sandbar, and the Domus Conversorum was later repurposed as a home for clerks — the expulsion stood until Oliver Cromwell's era. Domestically, Edward convoked the 1295 "Model Parliament," broadening representation to include lesser clergy and elected shire and borough representatives, and passed major legal reforms including the Statutes of Mortmain (barring land grants to the Church) and the Statute of Quia Emptores (banning subinfeudation, hastening the end of feudalism); he also imposed the unpopular wool tax called the "maltolt," clashed with Archbishop Robert Winchelsey over clerical taxation after Winchelsey published the papal bull Clericis Laicos, ordered the "Hundred Rolls" corruption census, and had his regent Robert Burnell pursue quo warranto proceedings against nobles' landholdings. After Eleanor of Castile died in 1290, Edward erected twelve memorial crosses along the route of her funeral procession from Harby to Westminster Abbey. He is also the title subject of George Peele's Elizabethan history play The Famous Chronicle of King Edward the First, which dramatizes his crusade and his Welsh and Scottish victories, ending with a fabricated scandal in which Queen Elinor confesses she did not father their daughter Joan.
recursive_suggestions: William Wallace — recurring standalone answerline as Edward's chief Scottish opponent; own History topic covering Stirling Bridge, Falkirk, and his execution
Robert the Bruce — Scottish leader who emerges after this era; watch for confusion since many Wars of Scottish Independence clues split between Edward I, Wallace, and Bruce

Edward II — Edward I's son and successor; extremely frequent namesake contamination in this search (5 tossups discarded — his reign, Piers Gaveston, Bannockburn, deposition/murder, and Marlowe's play are entirely separate content)
Edward III — Edward I's grandson; also namesake-contaminated this search (2 tossups discarded — Hundred Years' War, Crecy/Poitiers)
Edward IV — unrelated Yorkist king from the Wars of the Roses; heavily namesake-contaminated this search (3 tossups discarded)
Simon de Montfort — recurring answerline as leader of the baronial opposition in the Second Barons' War; distinct topic covering the Montfort Parliament and Battle of Evesham
Llywelyn ap Gruffudd (Llywelyn the Last) — recurring answerline as the last independent Prince of Wales; distinct topic from Edward's conquest
Eleanor of Castile — recurring answerline in her own right (Eleanor Crosses, crusade legend); could support a standalone entry
Piers Gaveston / Roger Mortimer / Isabella of France — recurring Edward II-era answerlines; excluded here as namesake contamination, not Edward I content

the Edwardian conquest of Wales — has independent question presence as its own answerline (1 tossup, 3 bonus parts this search); currently folded into this page's Conquest of Wales section but could be split out if it grows

expulsion of the Jews from England — has independent question presence as its own answerline (1 tossup, 2 bonus parts this search); currently folded into this page's Expulsion section but could be split out if it grows
Charles Ives / Charles Edward Ives — unrelated American composer; namesake-contaminated this search via first name/full name match (6 tossups, many bonus parts discarded)
Ned Kelly — unrelated Australian outlaw from a Literature question about Peter Carey's novel; discarded (answerline accepts "Edward" as a nickname substitute for "Ned")
